title: Workflow & State Modelling — Jira and Rally Patterns
topic: How do Jira and Rally model workflows / states / status flows for different work item types — applied to Vector's o_artefact_flow_default / o_subscription_artefact_flow design
summary: Jira workflow schemes + Rally Flow/Schedule/Portfolio state — patterns and recommendations for Vector's flow tables.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Jira Cloud — Workflows and Status Categories
  4. 4. Rally — Schedule State, Flow State, and Portfolio State
  5. 5. Defaulting Mechanism for Tenant-Created Types
  6. 6. Mapping Bespoke States to Canonical Categories
  7. 7. Comparison Matrix
  8. 8. Vector Recommendations
  9. 9. Sources
  10. 10. Gaps

1. Overview

Vector's migration 105 introduced o_artefact_flow_default (vendor seed) and o_subscription_artefact_flow (tenant copy, runtime-read), keyed on the registry artefact-type UUID with optional dispatch to portfolio_item_types. Two open questions emerged: (a) how do we provide vendor-seeded defaults for all artefact types, not just execution_work_items, and (b) how do we handle the fact that portfolio_item_types are tenant-created — vendor cannot pre-seed UUIDs that don't exist yet. This paper benchmarks the two industry leaders (Jira Cloud and Rally) and applies their patterns to Vector's schema.

The headline finding: Rally's two-tier Schedule State (workspace-canonical) → Flow State (project-bespoke, many-to-one mapped) is structurally identical to Vector's canonical_states → o_subscription_artefact_flow design — we are on the right track. But Rally also handles per-portfolio-type defaulting via a per-type default-states model with up to 20 values per type and four canonical seeds. Jira goes a different way: workflow schemes bind workflows to issue types with a default-workflow fallback for any type not explicitly mapped. Both patterns inform Vector's path forward.

2. Key Findings

  • Rally has a two-tier state model already — Schedule State (workspace-scoped, 4 fixed canonical values + 2 custom max) for stories/defects/tasks, and Flow State (project-scoped, ≤20 columns) that maps many-to-one back to Schedule State. This is exactly Vector's intended canonical_states → o_subscription_artefact_flow shape.
  • Rally Portfolio Items use a different model — each Portfolio Item Type has its own State field with up to 20 values; Rally seeds the same four defaults (Discovering / Developing / Measuring / Done) for every type but admins are expected to customise per type. Critically, there is no canonical mapping — Portfolio State values are free text without a clock-role or category tie-back.
  • Jira's status categories are fixed at three — every custom status must be tagged To Do, In Progress, or Done. You cannot create new categories; this is hard-coded. JQL and reporting tools rely on this for cross-project consistency. This validates Vector's approach of forcing every bespoke flow state to FK to canonical_states.code.
  • Jira solves "new custom issue type" via workflow schemes — a scheme maps issue types → workflows, with a "default workflow" fallback for any unmapped type. Adding a new issue type to a project that uses an existing scheme automatically inherits the default workflow until an admin explicitly remaps it.
  • Both tools rely on copy-on-create for tenant defaults — Jira project templates clone workflow schemes; Rally seeds default state values per Portfolio Item Type. Neither maintains a live link from defaults to tenant configuration; both are one-way copies that the tenant then owns.
  • Rally's caution: keep bespoke and canonical synchronised — Broadcom explicitly warns that "using other applications or boards to change flow state can cause the schedule state and flow state to become out of sync." Vector's design (single tenant table read at runtime, canonical_code FK enforced at write) avoids this drift entirely.

3. Jira Cloud — Workflows and Status Categories

3.1 The four moving parts

Jira separates its state model into four distinct objects:

ObjectScopeEditablePurpose
StatusGlobal (instance-wide)AdminNamed state (e.g. "Open", "In Review", "Stakeholder Sign-off")
Status CategoryGlobal, fixedNo — exactly three existBucket: To Do, In Progress, Done
WorkflowGlobal; can be active or inactiveAdmin (only when inactive)Set of statuses + transitions for a class of work
Workflow SchemeGlobal; bound to one or more projectsAdminMaps issue types → workflows; has one default workflow

3.2 How status categories work

Atlassian documentation: "statuses are classified into one of three categories: To Do, In Progress, or Done. These categories are color-coded… The category names and colors are not customizable." When a Jira admin creates a custom status they pick a name and pick one of the three categories — there is no option to add a fourth. JQL queries like statusCategory = "In Progress" rely on this fixed taxonomy to span heterogeneous project workflows.

This is structurally identical to Vector's canonical_states table: five fixed codes (defined, ready, in_progress, completed, accepted) with clock_role tags driving cycle/lead-time metrics. Jira chose three buckets; Vector chose five (richer because it splits "ready/in_progress" and adds an "accepted" terminal). Same concept.

3.3 How workflow schemes solve "new issue type"

The default-workflow attribute of a workflow scheme defines which workflow to use if an issue type is used without an explicit mapping. When an admin creates a new custom issue type and adds it to a project that already uses a scheme, that issue type automatically inherits the scheme's default workflow until the admin explicitly maps it elsewhere. This is the "soft default" pattern: no errors, no blank state — the new type just gets the project's default workflow until the admin chooses otherwise.

3.4 How workflows propagate across projects

Workflow schemes are shared, not copied. When admins create a new project using "shared configuration", the new project points at the same workflow scheme — changes to that scheme propagate to every project using it. This makes Jira's model live-shared by default, copy-on-fork by exception. Vector's flow_default → flow_tenant is the opposite: copy-on-create, no live link. Both work; the choice depends on whether vendor-driven updates should auto-propagate (Jira) or whether tenants own their flow once created (Vector — current design).

4. Rally — Schedule State, Flow State, and Portfolio State

4.1 Schedule State (canonical, workspace-scoped)

Rally's foundational state field for stories, defects, tasks, defect suites, test sets, and risks. Broadcom: "By default, you will have 'defined', 'in-progress', 'completed', and 'accepted'. These four states cannot be modified or disabled. You can, however, create up to two additional Schedule States." This is the canonical workspace-level vocabulary — the equivalent of Vector's canonical_states table — except Rally lets workspace admins extend it (max 6 total). Vector's canonical_states is not extensible; bespoke states live in the per-tenant flow table instead.

4.2 Flow State (bespoke, project-scoped, mapped many-to-one)

This is the design pattern most relevant to Vector. Quoting Broadcom's knowledge base:

  • "Flow State is a Rally built-in field created for the Team Board for mapping Schedule States to team-specific columns."
  • "Flow states are scoped to a project while schedule states are workspace-scoped."
  • "You can use up to 20 flow states on your Team Board page."
  • "When mapping columns to schedule states, you can map multiple columns to the same schedule state to represent your workflow. For example, many teams choose to have a Backlog column and a Ready to Pull column. You can map both of these columns to the Defined schedule state."

This is exactly Vector's design. o_subscription_artefact_flow.canonical_code performs the same many-to-one mapping role: a tenant can name a state "UI Review" or "QA Regression" but it must FK to one of the five canonical codes. Where Rally calls them "Flow States" tied to "Schedule States", Vector calls them "tenant flow states" tied to "canonical states". Same idea, different names.

4.3 Portfolio Item State (per-type, no canonical mapping)

The Portfolio side of Rally diverges. Broadcom: "You can modify the state fields for each portfolio item type in your hierarchy. You can have only 20 state values per type." The defaults are the same four for every Portfolio Item Type — Discovering / Developing / Measuring / Done — but admins are expected to customise per type (Theme states differ from Feature states in practice). Crucially, "State names are unique for a single type. You can reuse state names across multiple types but must define them for each type."

And there is no canonical mapping. Portfolio State values are free text — they don't tie back to Schedule State or any other canonical vocabulary. This is a deliberate Rally limitation, not a feature. The Portfolio Kanban board renders columns in rank order; cycle-time metrics are computed by tracking transitions between Portfolio State values directly, not by rolling them up to canonical buckets.

4.4 The Rally caution Vector should heed

Broadcom warns: "Using other applications or boards to change flow state can cause the schedule state and flow state to become out of sync." Rally's design has two writable surfaces (Flow State on the Team Board, Schedule State on the artefact form), and external tools that hit one but not the other create drift. Vector's design avoids this entirely: the runtime reads only o_subscription_artefact_flow, and every row's canonical_code is enforced by FK. There is no parallel writable surface.

5. Defaulting Mechanism for Tenant-Created Types

5.1 Jira's approach — workflow scheme + default workflow

When a Jira admin creates a new custom issue type:

  1. The issue type is added at the global instance level (it's just a row in the issue-type catalogue).
  2. For each project that should use it, the admin adds the issue type to the project's issue-type scheme.
  3. The project's workflow scheme handles state-flow assignment automatically: if the new type has no explicit mapping in the workflow scheme, it inherits the scheme's default workflow. No "blank state" is possible.
  4. If the admin wants the new type to use a different workflow, they create a new workflow (or copy an existing one) and add it to the workflow scheme as an explicit mapping for that type.

Jira's pattern: type-to-workflow is a many-to-one association resolved through a configurable scheme, with a guaranteed fallback.

5.2 Rally's approach — per-type seeded defaults, then admin-customise

When a Rally admin enables or configures a Portfolio Item Type:

  1. Rally provides the four default state values (Discovering / Developing / Measuring / Done) for every Portfolio Item Type out of the box.
  2. The admin is expected to customise per type ("As part of your portfolio management process, you should define different values from the defaults we provide, as well as default values set for each portfolio item type.")
  3. State values are owned per type — adding/renaming/reordering is per-type work.

For the schedulable artefacts (stories/defects/tasks/test cases), Rally is more rigid: Schedule State is fixed at workspace level with the four canonical values + up to 2 custom — the same vocabulary applies to all schedulable types. Per-team customisation happens via Flow State mapping, not by extending Schedule State per type.

5.3 Pattern comparison

SystemHow new artefact types get a starting flowFallback when no explicit configLive link to defaults?
JiraWorkflow scheme maps type → workflow; admin can copy an existing workflow as a starting pointScheme's default workflowYes — schemes are live-shared across projects
Rally (schedulable)All schedulable types share workspace-level Schedule State; teams customise via Flow State mappingWorkspace Schedule State appliesNo for Flow State (per-project copy)
Rally (portfolio)Each PI type seeded with same 4 defaults; admin customises per typeThe four seeded defaults apply if untouchedNo — copy-on-enable, then tenant-owned
Vector (current)o_artefact_flow_default seeds vendor templates; o_subscription_artefact_flow is tenant copyCurrently NONE — only execution_work_items seededNo — copy-on-create planned, not yet wired

6. Mapping Bespoke States to Canonical Categories

6.1 Jira's three-bucket enforcement

Jira's status-category model is the strictest of the three:

  • Three categories, fixed: To Do (grey), In Progress (blue), Done (green).
  • Cannot be extended. Cannot be renamed. Cannot be recoloured.
  • Every status — built-in or custom — must be tagged with exactly one category at creation time.
  • Reporting (JQL, dashboards, gadgets) all reference statusCategory for cross-project, cross-workflow queries.

This is a deliberate trade-off: Jira sacrifices flexibility for guaranteed cross-project comparability. A query like "show me everything in progress across the entire instance" works regardless of whether one team uses a 5-state workflow and another uses a 12-state one.

6.2 Rally's two answers

For schedulable items: Flow State → Schedule State mapping is enforced at the field-definition level. When an admin creates a Flow State column, they pick the Schedule State it maps to from a closed dropdown of the workspace's Schedule State values. The mapping is stored as a foreign-key-style reference; the runtime synchronises Schedule State whenever a Flow State changes (provided changes go through Rally's API).

For Portfolio items: no mapping exists. Portfolio State is free text and is its own canonical vocabulary per type. This is a known Rally limitation — Portfolio Kanban metrics live in their own world, isolated from the Schedule State metrics engine.

6.3 Vector's design (already in place)

o_subscription_artefact_flow.canonical_code TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES canonical_states(code) ON DELETE RESTRICT enforces the same closed-mapping that Rally's Flow State has, but uniformly across all artefact types — schedulable and portfolio. This is the strongest design of the three: a single canonical vocabulary, FK-enforced, applied to every flow state regardless of artefact type, with bespoke names freely customisable. Jira's three-bucket model is the closest analogue but Vector's is finer-grained (5 codes, with explicit clock_role tags for metrics).

7. Comparison Matrix

ConcernJira CloudRally (schedulable)Rally (portfolio)Vector (current)Vector (proposed)
Canonical vocabulary3 fixed categories4 fixed Schedule States + 2 custom maxNone — per-type free text5 canonical codes (immutable)Unchanged
Bespoke state layerCustom statuses tagged with categoryFlow States (≤20 per project, mapped to ScheduleState)Per-type State (≤20 per type)o_subscription_artefact_flow rowsUnchanged
Mapping enforcementStatus → category at creation (closed)Flow → Schedule via dropdown (closed)NoneFK to canonical_states.code (closed)Unchanged
Per-type vs sharedPer workflow scheme (many types may share one workflow)Shared across all schedulable typesPer typePer type (registry) or per portfolio_item_typeUnchanged
Vendor defaultsImplicit via project templatesWorkspace-level defaults (4 immutable)Same 4 defaults seeded per typeOnly execution_work_items seededSeed all 6 registry types + portfolio archetype
New tenant type starting flowDefault workflow in schemeInherits workspace Schedule StateInherits the 4 default valuesNone — admin must define from scratchCopy from portfolio archetype on type creation
Update propagationLive-shared schemesWorkspace updates affect all projectsTenant-owned after creationTenant-owned after creationUnchanged (copy-on-create, no auto-propagate)
Max bespoke statesNo documented hard cap20 per project20 per typeNo cap (recommend 20 to match)Add CHECK or app-level cap at 20
Cycle-time / lead-time engineJQL on statusCategorySchedule State transitionsFree-text — separate Portfolio Kanban metricscanonical_states.clock_role + item_state_historyUnchanged

8. Vector Recommendations

8.1 Adopt Rally's "per-type defaults" pattern for the 6 registry types

Migration 105 only seeded execution_work_items. Add a follow-up migration seeding default flows for all 6 registry types (these are vendor-known and stable):

Registry scope_keySuggested default flow (positions → bespoke name → canonical_code)
execution_work_items1=Defined→defined, 2=To Do→ready, 3=Doing→in_progress, 4=Completed→completed, 5=Accepted→accepted (already seeded)
execution_defects1=Reported→defined, 2=Triaged→ready, 3=In Progress→in_progress, 4=Fixed→completed, 5=Verified→accepted
execution_tasks1=To Do→ready, 2=Doing→in_progress, 3=Done→completed (3-state simple flow)
execution_test_cases1=Draft→defined, 2=Ready→ready, 3=Passing→completed, 4=Failing→in_progress, 5=Blocked→in_progress (multiple states tying to in_progress)
execution_epics1=Defined→defined, 2=Ready→ready, 3=In Progress→in_progress, 4=Completed→completed, 5=Accepted→accepted (story-shaped)
strategic1=Discovering→defined, 2=Developing→in_progress, 3=Measuring→in_progress, 4=Done→completed (Rally-shape)

This is the Rally-portfolio pattern: vendor seeds sensible per-type defaults; tenant overrides on subscription creation; admin customises after.

8.2 Solve the "tenant-created portfolio_item_type" problem with archetype defaults

Vendor cannot seed defaults keyed by portfolio_item_type UUID (UUIDs don't exist until tenant creates them). Two options:

  1. Archetype model (recommended) — add a portfolio_item_archetype TEXT enum to the system catalogue (e.g. strategic_container, delivery_unit, discovery_artefact). Each archetype has its own seeded default flow. When a gadmin creates a new portfolio_item_type, they pick an archetype, and on commit the system clones that archetype's flow into o_subscription_artefact_flow for the new type's UUID. This mirrors Rally's "all PI types start with the same 4 defaults" pattern but lets Vector ship more than one shape (a Theme-shaped flow differs sensibly from a Feature-shaped flow).
  2. Single shared portfolio default — like Rally, give every new portfolio_item_type the same 4-state seed (Discovering / Developing / Measuring / Done) and force the admin to customise. Simpler schema, worse UX.

Recommendation: option 1 (archetype) — adds one catalogue table and one nullable column on portfolio_item_types, gives much better out-of-box UX, costs ~30 lines of seed.

8.3 Keep copy-on-create (do not adopt Jira's live-shared model)

Jira's live-shared workflow scheme model has a known headache: vendor or admin updates to a shared scheme propagate instantly to every project, surprising teams who didn't expect their flow to change. Vector's flow_default → flow_tenant copy-on-create is the safer pattern (Rally also chose copy). Tenants own their flow once provisioned; library DB updates to flow_default can be surfaced as a "new defaults available — review changes?" gadmin notification, not auto-applied.

8.4 Add a 20-state cap per (tenant, target)

Both Rally tiers cap at 20 (per project for Flow State, per type for Portfolio State). Vector should match — add a partial-index trigger or app-layer check that errors if a tenant tries to create a 21st state for a given (subscription_id, target). Prevents pathological tenant configurations that would slow board renders.

8.5 Add a "review your defaults" surface for gadmins

When library DB updates o_artefact_flow_default (e.g. vendor adds a sixth default state for execution_test_cases), surface a gadmin notification: "Library defaults for Test Cases changed — view diff." Gadmin can review and selectively apply, mirroring Jira's "shared scheme changed" awareness without forcing auto-propagation.

8.6 Defer: workflow transitions

Both Jira and Rally model transitions (which states can move to which other states). Vector's item_type_transition_edges table (migration 006) already exists for this but is unused. Do not wire transitions in this migration — keep flow-state plumbing focused. Transitions are a separate phase 4 concern, after states are read at runtime by the artefact endpoints.

9. Sources

10. Gaps

  • Jira workflow-scheme inheritance fine print — Atlassian's docs describe the default-workflow fallback but don't precisely document what happens when a workflow scheme's default workflow is changed while issues already exist in unmapped types (do those issues stay on the old workflow or transition?). Likely tenant-specific; not load-bearing for Vector since we're not adopting live-shared schemes.
  • Rally's exact transition-edge model for Portfolio State — TechDocs describes adding/reordering Portfolio State values but doesn't fully document whether transitions are implicit (any → any) or restricted. Does not affect Vector's design since transitions are deferred.
  • Jira's status-category enforcement at the API layer — could not confirm via the docs whether a custom status created via REST API can omit a category or whether the API forces one. Marketplace apps reportedly have workarounds. Inferred from UI behaviour that category is mandatory.
  • Per-tenant performance characteristics at scale — neither Jira nor Rally publishes how many statuses/Flow States large customers maintain in practice. The 20-state cap is documented for Rally; Jira has no documented hard cap. Vector's recommended 20-state cap is conservative but not benchmarked.
